home.whatWeDo: Through the HHS Cyber Gateway, you will have access to the latest information, dedicated HPH cybersecurity resources and tools to bolster your organization's cybersecurity defenses. Whether you are a small clinic or a large healthcare system, we are committed to supporting you in your efforts to safeguard patient data and ensure the integrity and availability of critical healthcare services
home.whoWeAre: The increasing sophistication of cyber threats demands a united front from the HPH sector. As the Sector Risk Management Agency, we have launched The HHS Cyber Gateway in direct response to this imperative, providing a curated selection of resources tailored to meet the unique cybersecurity needs of healthcare organizations. By leveraging the collective expertise of federal agencies and collaborating with industry stakeholders, we believe this website will help empower HPH Sector members navigate the evolving threat landscape effectively.
home.howCanWeHelpYou: The HHS Cyber Gateway cornerstone publications provide your organizations with proven best practices to implement and use to mitigate the most prevalent and emerging cyber threats of today. We provide FREE cyber hygiene resources to educate small to large organizations on how to stay cyber aware and increase their cyber preparedness. Our vetted publications and supporting resources have one goal in mind, to keep patient safety a top priority - remember: “Cyber Safety is Patient Safety”!
home.engageWithUs: ZZZ The 405(d) Program is continuously growing! In addition to products like HICP, the Program releases content through a variety of channels that offer best practices and tips for organizations small to large to implement to protect patients and organizations. Follow our newsletter and social channels to stay up to date on latest news and events from the program!

cornerstone.descr: The 405(d) Program develops consensus-based best practices and methodologies to strengthen the Healthcare & Public Health (HPH) sector’s cybersecurity posture against cyber threats. Through partnerships between government and industry, the program has released TWO vetted landmark publications for the sector to utilize in their cybersecurity defense. The 405(d) Program and Task Group actively continues to develop new products to help further strengthen our sector.
cornerstone.hicp: Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices: Managing Threats and Protecting Patients (HICP 2023 Edition) outlines the top threats facing the HPH Sector. Developed with every stakeholder in mind, organizations ranging in size from small to large can benefit from the resources and best practices provided in the main document and additional two technical volumes. HICP aims to provide organizations with recommendations and best practices to prepare and fight against cybersecurity threats that can impact patient safety.
cornerstone.occi: The Operational Continuity-Cyber Incident (OCCI) checklist serves as an action plan designed to assist operational staff and executive management in effectively responding to and recovering from an extended enterprise outage caused by a severe cyber-attack. This checklist, an integral component of your broader Incident Response Plan (IRP), is specifically tailored to address the critical first 12 hours following a cyber event.
cornerstone.landscape: The Hospital Resiliency Landscape Analysis conducted a deeper investigative study into both methods that cyber adversaries are using to compromise US hospitals, disrupt operations and extort them for financial gain. It then benchmarked these results to specific practices in the Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices (HICP) in order to outline the most meaningful protections for these specific threats.

kod.descr: Knowledge on Demand is our free cybersecurity education platform. It includes multiple levels of delivery methodologies designed to reach the varied size health care facilities across the country. Our platform includes cybersecurity awareness trainings that align with the top 5 cybersecurity threats outlined in the landmark Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices publication.

carouselItems.title: Five Threats
longDescription: Our Five Threat series is where our knowledge on demand platform was born. These cybersecurity training videos align with the top five cybersecurity threats outlined in Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices. These modules are designed for your healthcare staff, security team, and any other department on the front line  to protect patient safety.
Don’t forget to download our Knowledge on Demand Five Threat Series Quiz to go along with your modules!

carouselItems.title: Quick Shots
carouselItems.longDescription: Quick Shots are designed to be ‘quick’ two to three minute long videos that highlight key cybersecurity terminologies. These videos cover a wide variety of cybersecurity topics that impact the Healthcare and Public Health sector and provide information and definitions of important cybersecurity themes, strategies and more.

carouselItems.title: Cyber, Preparedness, Resilience
carouselItems.longDescription: CPR offers 30-minute interactive videos with knowledge checks that provide in-depth awareness trainings on a variety of cybersecurity topics. Each video highlights mitigations and best practices that improve cyber resiliency within organizations of all sizes.

carouselItems.title: Prescribed For You
carouselItems.longDescription: Prescribed for You will be role-based awareness trainings that focus on individual roles in the healthcare sector. These one-to-two hour interactive sessions will dive deeper into cybersecurity topics and how they relate to specific roles, providing direct mitigation techniques and best practices.

carouselItems.title: Advanced Education Development
carouselItems.longDescription: The AED series will feature online instructor led courses that span over the course of several weeks. The length will depend on the complexity of the topic. These courses will be led by industry and federal leaders, and will provide our community an opportunity to stay at the forefront of cybersecurity education and knowledge. Two programs will be offered per year and will include topics such as a Cyber Leadership and Zero Trust Strategies.

fiveThreats.descr: Knowledge on Demand (KOD) is a cybersecurity education platform that includes multiple delivery methodologies to reach the varied size health care facilities across the country. Five cybersecurity trainings that align with the top five cybersecurity threats outlined in HICP are featured for training your healthcare staff, security team, and any other department that is on the front lines for protecting patient safety. The best part about this resource? It’s FREE!
fiveThreats.jobAid: These are single documents with key tips related to the topic. This format is meant to be used as an “on-the-job” resource tool. They can provide instructional steps if necessary to meet the training objectives.
fiveThreats.video: These videos are launched from the 405(d) KOD webpage. They include recorded audio to take the trainee through the video along with interactive content to include knowledge checks and animations.
fiveThreats.powerPoint: These can be leveraged for in person or on-site presentations. These will include facilitator notes with slide specific content and knowledge checks to reinforce learning. Such presentations can be delivered in presentation mode or in a “Lunch n Learn” format at your location.
fiveThreats.lms: Content intended for a Learning Management System (LMS) will be similar in look and experience to the previously discussed Interactive Training video. Content will be exported and saved to a file type compatible for import to an organization’s LMS platform.

descr: The Quick Shot video series are two to three minutes in length and share key cybersecurity terminology that impacts the Healthcare and Public Health  sector in a fun and engaging manner. These videos empower all HPH stakeholders to identify, protect against, and prevent cybersecurity threats. This series currently aligns to Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices and will expand to align with the other 405(d) Cornerstone Publications.
**Remember, a Quick Shot a day keeps the cybercriminal away!

The CPR video series are engaging modules that walks the trainee through in-depth cyber security best practices and more. These modules include interactive content such as knowledge checks along the way to help learners better grasp the content being presented. Each video highlights how to mitigate cyber threats and best practices that improve cyber resiliency within organizations of all sizes.
These videos can both be viewed on our website or downloaded for later viewing.

prescribedForYou.descr: Prescribed for You will be role-based awareness trainings that focus on individual roles in the healthcare sector. These one-to-two hour interactive sessions will dive deeper into cybersecurity topics and how they relate to specific roles, which will highlight direct mitigation techniques and best practices.

aed.descr: The AED series will feature online instructor-led courses that span over the course of several weeks, length will depend on the complexity of the topic. These courses will be led by industry and federal leaders, and will provide our community an opportunity to stay at the forefront of cybersecurity education and knowledge. Two programs will be o¬ffered per year and will include topics such as a Cyber Leadership and Zero Trust Strategies.

post.descr: Our bi-monthly newsletter strives to educate our stakeholders on new and emerging cybersecurity threats and technologies by highlighting the use of the HICP publication, how our Task Group members have used it in practical situations, and share news of upcoming cybersecurity and U.S. Department of Health and Human Services events.

resources.descr: Our cybersecurity resources are free and can be used by organizations sized small to large. Search below to find the latest best practices, cyber hygiene resources, and more! Search and use the bank of resources HHS has to offer to find information and materials in one place!
resources.archiveDescr: Find older 405(d) publications, hygiene tips, and resources to protect patients from cybersecurity threats in our archived resources that doesn’t contain the latest and updated content.

landscapeAnalysis.top: The HPH Sector has faced dramatic increases in cyber-attacks intended to cause disruption to the care continuum. In response to this growing threat, the HHS 405(d) Program in partnership Health Sector Coordinating Council Cybersecurity Working Group (HSCC CWG), and the HHS’ Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) conducted Landscape Analysis, which reviewed active threats attacking hospitals and the cybersecurity capabilities of hospitals operating in the United States.

descr: This report aims to discuss the dramatic increases in cyber-attacks to the HPH Sector intended to cause disruption to the care continuum. The Landscape Analysis conducted a deeper investigative study into both the methods that cyber adversaries are using to compromise US hospitals, disrupt operations and extort for financial gain. It then benchmarked these results to specific practices of the Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices (HICP) in order to outline the most meaningful protections to these specific threats.
objectives: To develop a clear understanding of the current cybersecurity capabilities and preparedness across participating U.S. hospitals, as well as their ability to combat cyber threats

To share the analysis and findings with the HSCC CWG for consideration as one of several inputs for informing prioritized cybersecurity practices for U.S. hospitals, as well as other considerations the U.S. government might undertake to improve U.S. hospitals’ cybersecurity resiliency

threatsIdentified.contents: The Landscape Analysis included a review of active threats attacking hospitals and the cybersecurity capabilities of U.S. hospitals. Included within the Landscape Analysis are the results of investigations into:
threats: The tactics and techniques that threat actors use to compromise hospitals
The current state of participating hospital cybersecurity resiliency (using the Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices (HICP) as a framework).

threatAnalysis.descr: The assessment, based on the data sources used, identified numerous cybersecurity threats to U.S. hospitals such as:
threats: Cloud exploitations by threat actors with data suggesting a 95% increase from 2021 to cloud exploitations cases
Phishing/Spear-Phishing attacks, specifically those attacks that overcome MFA through social engineering

keyTakeAways: Human Directed Attacks make up 71% of attacks
Access Broker theft up 112% used by the human directed attacks
Time to move off initial intrusion point is 1 hour 28 minutes (this is the lateral movement off the originally compromised host to another state to obfuscate)

keyObservations.descr: The analysis from the two (2) quantitative studies combined with participating hospital conversations resulted in a series of 10 key observations:
observations: Directly targeted ransomware attacks aimed to disrupt clinical operations are an outsized and growing cyber threat to hospitals

observations.text: Variable adoption of critical security features and processes, coupled with a continually evolving threat landscape can expose hospitals to more cyber-attacks
list: Adoption of MFA is taking place in over 90% of surveyed hospitals- why not 100%?
89% of the hospitals surveyed indicated that they were conducting regular vulnerability scanning at least on a quarterly basis
86% of the hospitals surveyed responded that their users are informed and trained on performing their cybersecurity related duties and responsibilities
The delivery of in-home care, accelerated by COVID-19, is growing and expanding the cyber threat landscape

observations: Hospitals report measurable success in implementing email protections, which is a key attack vector
Supply chain risk is pervasive for hospitals. Only 49% of hospitals state they have adequate coverage in managing risks to supply chain risk management
Medical devices have not typically been exploited to disrupt clinical operations in hospital
There is significant variation in cybersecurity resiliency among hospitals

observations.text: The use of antiquated hardware, systems, and software by hospitals is concerning
list: 96% of small, medium, and large sized hospitals claim they were operating with end-of-life operating systems or software with known vulnerabilities, which is inclusive of medical devices.

observations.text: Cybersecurity insurance premiums continue to rise
list: On average, cybersecurity premiums increased by 46% in 2021. Five of fifty-six hospitals surveyed in 2022 experienced increases more than 100%, whereas 32 experienced increases just below 35%.

observations: Securing cyber talent with requisite skills and experience is challenging
keyObservations.bottomDescr: An interesting correlation that was uncovered during analysis was a strong connection between those who have adopted HICP and robust NIST CSF coverage. This indicates that organizations that focus on HICP Practices will gain value and benefit towards managing implementation of the NIST CSF cybersecurity framework.
keyObservations.footNotes: NOTE: For the purposes of this study, the scope was narrowed to those activities that protect access to patient care and safety and reduce the negative impact of cyber threats on clinical operations. Breaches of sensitive data, although equally important, are not the focus of this study - unless the breach has a direct impact on patient care and safety

dataSets.title: CHIME Data
list: 177 hospitals representing small (owning just 1 hospital)
107 hospitals representing medium (owning between 2 and 5 hospitals
87 hospitals representing large (owning more than 5 hospitals)

dataSets.title: Censinet/AHA/KLAS Study
list: 59 small, medium and large hospitals (size measured by # of beds - using HICP), evaluating on coverage to the NIST CSF and 405(d) HICP, as well as organized benchmarking

dataSets.title: 2023 H-ISAC Threst Study
list: 11 notable threat actors profiled with descriptions of their tactics, techniques and procedures
288 heapthcare executives surveyed to determine top threats

dataSets.title: HC3 Threat Data
list: 2,224 healthcare specific cybersecurity incidents
Deep analysis of 33 FBI, CISA and HC3 Threat Analysis Reports

dataSets.title: Verizon 2022 DBIR Report
list: 23,896 security incidents across a verity of sectors
5.212 confirmed data breaches of the 23,896 incidents
849 incidents and 571 confirmed data disclosure in the healthcare sector

practiceAdoption.title: No Action Required - Significant Progress Made
list: E-mail protection systems

practiceAdoption.title: Urgent Improvement Needed
list: Endpoint Protection Systems
Identity and Access MAnagement
Network Management
Vulnerability Management
Security Operation Center and Incident Response

practiceAdoption.title: Additional Research Required
list: IT Asset Management
Network Connected MEdical Devices Security
Cybersecurity Oversight and Governance

practiceAdoption.title: Further Attention Required (Not Urgent)
list: Data Protection and Loss Prevention

spanish.descr: El Programa 405(d) ha dado el paso inicial específico para llegar a los usuarios finales en el Sector HPH que usan el español como lengua materna o se sienten más cómodos usando el español que el inglés. Los recursos específicos del programa se han traducido al español para comunicar la ciberhigiene en un entorno médico.

items.title: Resumen de guías de recursos
items.descr: Estos se pueden aprovechar para presentaciones en persona o en el sitio. Estos incluirán notas del facilitador con contenido específico de diapositivas y comprobaciones de conocimientos para reforzar el aprendizaje. Estas presentaciones se pueden realizar en modo de presentación o en formato “Lunch n Learn” en su ubicación.

items.title: Carteles
items.descr: Estos carteles se centran en la ciberhigiene diaria para ayudar a fortalecer su enfoque de ciberseguridad. Explore cada póster que puede utilizar en su propio entorno de atención médica para ayudar a proteger a los pacientes de las amenazas cibernéticas.

items.title: Recursos sobre amenazas cibernéticas
items.descr: Se trata de documentos individuales con consejos clave relacionados con el tema. Este formato está destinado a ser utilizado como una herramienta de recursos an “en el trabajo”. Pueden proporcionar pasos de instrucción si es necesario para cumplir con los objetivos de capacitación.

OcciChecklist.top: The intent of this OCCI Checklist is to provide organizations of all sizes with key actionable and vetted steps that can be put into place at the first sign of a large-scale cybersecurity incident. Whether you are a small organization or a larger integrated organization, the OCCI Checklist is organized from a role-based perspective. Each role aligns to tasks which an assigned staff member would be responsible for implementing in real time, within an HPH setting. In smaller health organizations, these critical roles may often be assigned as secondary functions, and the OCCI checklist is strategically designed to offer them comprehensive guidance on high-level responsibilities and actionable steps, should they find themselves assuming these roles during a cyber incident.

roles.title: Incident Commander
roles.descr: Provides overall strategic direction on all site-specific response actions and activities.

roles.title: Medical-Technical Specialist (Subject Matter Expert/Advisor)
roles.descr: Subject matter expert(s) who advises the Incident Commander or Section Chief on issues related to response; provides understanding and communicates specific impact and recommendations given their area of expertise.

roles.title: Public Information Officer
roles.descr: Serve as the conduit for information to internal and external stakeholders, including site personnel, visitors and families, and the news media, as approved by Cybersecurity, IT/IS Section Chief and the Incident Commander.

roles.title: Liaison
roles.descr: Function as the cyber incident contact for the Command Center for representatives from federal, state or local agencies.

roles.title: Safety Officer
roles.descr: Identify, monitor, and mitigate safety risks to patients, staff, and visitors during a prolonged large-scale outage.

roles.title: Operations Section Chief
roles.descr: Develop and recommend strategies and tactics to continue clinical and non-clinical operations for the duration of the cyber incident response and for recovery.

roles.title: Planning Section Chief
roles.descr: Oversee all cyber incident related documentation regarding cyber incident operations and resource management; initiate long range planning; conduct planning meetings; prepare the Incident Action Plan (IAP) for each operational period.

roles.title: Finance Section Chief
roles.descr: Monitor the utilization of financial assets and the accounting for financial expenditures; supervise the documentation of expenditures and cost reimbursement activities.

roles.title: Logistics Section Chief
roles.descr: Organize and direct the service and support activities needed to ensure material needs for the site’s response to a cyber incident are available when needed.

roles.title: Information Technologies (IT) / Information Systems (IS) Section Chief
roles.descr: Provide technical response, continuity, and recovery recommendations; partner with cybersecurity to inform cyber incident response decisions and activities. Coordinates intelligence and investigation efforts.f

usingChecklist: This document is meant to recommend or inform action from the Command team for the first twelve hours of the cyber incident.
The document was developed in alignment with the Hospital / Healthcare Incident Command System (HICS) and includes recommended actions
for Incident Command positions.
**An organization should**:
Review the checklist and begin to identify who in their organization should perform these roles
1. The checklist should be distributed and reviewed to help make all participants
are aware of their responsibilities. The urgency of each task should be
communicated, as the checklist is more impactful if all participants are
engaged and responsive.
2. When possible, identify a primary and secondary for each role.
3. Display the checklist in a pertinent location that is easily accessible and include
it in an onboarding package to be distributed to new staff joining the HPH
organization.
4. Review and update the checklist at least annually or after any cyber incident
to revise or update the process. To assist with the accuracy and operationality
of this resource, it should be exercised, and evaluated on a regular basis.
Exercises should be driven by measurable objectives and can be completed
at the team, department, or facility level. Examples of exercises could include
workshops, tabletop exercises, simulation drills, or functional / full scale
exercises. A debrief or after-action review should be completed post exercise
in which opportunities and action items are identified to facilitate ongoing
maturity and improvement of response processes and this plan.
OcciChecklist.howToUse: The purpose of this document is to serve as a resource guide and support Incident Command Activation and response actions in the case of a Cybersecurity Incident resulting in an extended downtime event. Incident Command Activation, IRP for a cyber incident, refers to the process of formally initiating and organizing the response efforts when a cybersecurity incident occurs. This document is meant to recommend or inform action from the Command team for the first twelve hours of the cyber incident.
OcciChecklist.tearaway: This publication contains a “Tearaways” Section which aims to give the designated stakeholders quick and easy access to the vital information laid out in this publication. We have carefully delineated each role along with their corresponding actions and created a tearaway page for them to use during these cyber events. These pages are meant to be taken out of the larger publication for easy access during an event for Incident Command positions.

information.howCanWeHelpYou: The 405(d) Program aims to develop consensus-based best practices and methodologies to strengthen the healthcare & public health (HPH) sector's cybersecurity posture against cyber threats. After significant analysis of the current cybersecurity issues facing the healthcare industry, the 405(d) Task Group developed Health Industry Cybersecurity Practice: Managing Threat and Protecting Patients, its first official Task Group product. The 405(d) Program and Task Group actively continues to develop new products to help further strengthen our sector.

role-it.title: IT professionals
role-it.descr: IT professionals can encounter a wide array of threats from social engineering to ransomware and may not have the “know how” to resolve every issue. Resources like the fact sheet and publications can help guide IT professionals with recommended cybersecurity practices to keep patient’s safe.

role-hp.title: healthcare practitioners
role-hp.descr: Healthcare practitioners are essential in the defense and fight against cybersecurity threats. Tools and resources are available for smaller sized practitioners that may not have the manpower of a large organization.

links.title: Staying Cyber Safe in the HPH Sector
Have You Heard - Protecting Medical Devices

role-ax.title: Administrators
role-ax.descr: Administrators can wear many hats in an organization. One of the most important roles is keeping patients safe from cybersecurity threats. Resources like the quick start guides in HICP are great to refer to for best practices or finding what you can do now to protect your patients.

org-sm.name: small
org-sm.descr: If you are considered a small organization, typically you have a smaller budget for cybersecurity mitigation as well as a smaller staff overall dedicated to cybersecurity. The 405(d) Program offers resources made just for you like the quick start guides and recommends sharing information with your security officer or provider. Check out the many resources that fit your organization!

org-md.name: medium
org-md.descr: If your organization wouldn’t be listed under large organizations but is not small either, you would fall into the medium organization section. A medium sized organization offers some resources and staffing when it comes to cybersecurity mitigation but is still constrained. Resources like HICP main document and Tech Volume 2 offers organizations options that fit their organization size to fight cybersecurity threats.

org-lg.name: large
org-lg.descr: Large organizations usually can afford a dedicated security team to protect patients from cybersecurity threats. Even a fully dedicated staff needs direction, recommendations, and helpful tips that fit their sized organization. Resources like the HICP main document and the large practices executive cards are great for your team to strengthen cybersecurity mitigation tactics.

hicp: Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices: Managing Threats and Protecting Patients (HICP 2023 Edition) outlines the top threats facing the HPH Sector. Developed with every stakeholder in mind, organizations from small to large can benefit from the resources and best practices provided in the main document and the additional two technical volumes. HICP aims to provide organizations with recommendations and best practices to prepare and fight against cybersecurity threats that can impact patient safety.
Do you want to start implementing HICP today in your organization?  Begin your cyber resilience journey with our interactive “How-to” map and get started today!
information.threats: The top five threats defined in HICP are social engineering, ransomware, loss or theft of equipment, insider, accidental, or malicious data loss, and attacks against network connected medical devices. Learn more about the 5 threats facing HPH and the early warnings that you can look for.
information.practices: Ranging from personnel training and awareness to the development and implementation of new processes, the 10 Cybersecurity Practices outlined in HICP Technical Volumes 1 & 2 provide best practices to mitigate the Top 5 Threats. Learn more on how you can start preparing and fighting cybersecurity threats today.
landscape: The HPH Sector has faced dramatic increases in cyber-attacks intended to cause disruption to the care continuum. In response to this growing threat, the HHS 405(d) Program conducted Landscape Analysis, which reviewed active threats attacking hospitals and the cybersecurity capabilities of hospitals operating in the United States.

The Landscape Analysis conducted a deeper investigative study into both the methods that cyber adversaries are using to compromise US hospitals, disrupt operations and extort for financial gain. It then benchmarked these results to specific practices of the Health Industry Cybersecurity Practices (HICP) in order to outline the most meaningful protections to these specific threats.
